summary: This lesson covers noun gender exceptions: Greek-origin words ending in -ma that are masculine (el problema, el sistema, el esquema), the feminine word mano (hand), and abbreviated feminines like foto and moto. It also introduces demonstratives este/esta (this) and ese/esa (that), and practices conditional structures with could/should.

teachings.summary: Many Spanish words ending in -ma entered through Greek. In Greek they are neuter but look masculine, so they became masculine in Spanish despite ending in -a. Examples: el problema, el sistema, el paradigma, el esquema, el diagrama, el planeta.
teachings.rule: Words ending in -ma that came from Greek are masculine: el problema, el sistema, el diagrama, el esquema, el paradigma, el planeta.
teachings.notes: El esquema follows the e-prosthesis rule (scheme -> esquema, since Spanish can't start with s+consonant).

teachings.summary: Este (masculine) and esta (feminine) mean 'this'. Remove the t to get ese/esa meaning 'that'. Plurals: estos/estas (these), esos/esas (those).
teachings.rule: This: este (m), esta (f). That: ese (m), esa (f). Plurals add -s: estos, estas, esos, esas. Remove t from este/esta to get ese/esa.

examples.spanish: No quiero ese auto, porque es muy grande.
examples.english: I don't want that car, because it's very big.

teachings.conceptName: La mano - feminine word ending in -o
teachings.summary: Mano (hand) is the only true feminine word ending in -o. La foto and la moto are not true exceptions - they are abbreviations of la fotografía and la motocicleta, retaining the feminine article.
teachings.rule: La mano = the hand. Feminine despite ending in -o. La foto = la fotografía. La moto = la motocicleta.
teachings.notes: Mano appears in English words: manuscript, manual, maintain (mantener), manage (manejar). Manejar means to handle/manage and also 'to drive' in some Latin American countries.

teachings.summary: Practiced conditional structures: could (podría), should (debería), would like (me gustaría), with various sentence constructions.

examples.english: Could you give me a hand?
You should give me a hand.
You guys should give me a hand.
You guys should give us a hand.

examples.spanish: Me gustaría tomar una foto, pero no tengo mi cámara.
examples.english: I would like to take a photo, but I don't have my camera.

teachings.summary: Tomar means to take. Used for 'tomar una foto' (to take a photo). Sacar can also be used for photos.

examples.english: I would like to take a photo.

teachings.notes: Sacar can also be used for taking photos: estoy sacando una foto.

vocabulary.derivation: found in manuscript, manual, maintain, manage

vocabulary.english: to handle, to manage, to drive
vocabulary.derivation: from mano (hand); originally for handling horses
